scenario: A polling pattern: keep calling an external API until response says "READY," then continue. The team is choosing between: Standard Loop activity (BPMN's "while-loop" marker on an activity). Worker-side loop in the Service Task's code (the worker internally retries until READY, returning only when ready).
question: Which approach better fits "poll until ready" in Camunda 8?

options.text: Worker-side loop (or explicit BPMN loop via Gateway + back-edge sequence flow) is the only viable pattern in Camunda 8. md:127-139 -- the <LoopSvg /> symbol carries no className="implemented" flag, in contrast to neighbouring symbols (Multi-Instance, Compensation) which do. Deployment-time validation rejects activities carrying <bpmn:standardLoopCharacteristics>. " → if yes continue, if no back-edge to Timer + Service Task. The explicit loop is visible in the diagram; worker-internal is more compact.

Standard Loop activity always works reliably for "poll until ready" scenarios in Zeebe -- this overstates the Standard Loop marker's availability. md:127-139, the <LoopSvg /> symbol carries no className="implemented" marker, in contrast to neighbouring symbols like Multi-Instance and Compensation which do. Deployment-time validation rejects activities carrying <bpmn:standardLoopCharacteristics>. Teams should instead use worker-side loops (internal polling in the job worker code) or explicit BPMN gateway back-edge loops.

Camunda 8 has no pattern for polling an external API until a condition is met -- this understates the available approaches. Worker-side polling (the job worker internally loops until the external system reports ready) and explicit BPMN loops (Service Task → XOR Gateway → Timer Intermediate Event → back-edge) are both well-supported patterns. Multiple well-documented strategies exist for implementing "wait and check" behaviour in Camunda 8, and teams routinely build polling processes using either approach.

Use Multi-Instance subprocess with a completionCondition to model the poll loop -- this is an awkward fit for unbounded polling scenarios. Multi-Instance works well for known-cardinality parallel processing, but for "poll until READY" with an indeterminate number of attempts, it requires artificially setting a high maximum cardinality and breaking early via the completionCondition. This approach is less intuitive, harder to read in the BPMN diagram, and harder to maintain than an explicit BPMN back-edge loop or a worker-side retry pattern.

a.text: Correct. "Poll until ready" patterns in Camunda 8: Pattern 1: Worker-side loop (compact) Service Task's worker code internally polls external API with a delay between attempts; returns when READY (or fails after max iterations). Pros: compact; one BPMN activity; worker controls the loop. Cons: hides the polling from BPMN readers; worker holds the job activation throughout (long-running worker). Pattern 2: BPMN-level explicit loop ` [Service Task: Check Status] → [XOR Gateway: ready?] → yes → [continue downstream] → no → [Timer Intermediate Event: wait 30s] → [back to Check Status] Pros: visible in BPMN; per-iteration observability (each "Check Status" execution in Operate); explicit timer between attempts. Cons: more elements; back-edge can look messy. Pattern 3: Standard Loop marker (NOT IMPLEMENTED) BPMN spec: with a loop condition. Zeebe does NOT implement Standard Loop per bpmn-coverage.md:127-139 -- lacks the className="implemented" marker. Deployment of activities carrying standardLoopCharacteristics` is rejected. For Camunda 8 production, Pattern 2 (explicit BPMN loop) is most robust + observable; Pattern 1 is compact but hides polling; Pattern 3 cannot be deployed.

b.text: Incorrect. Standard Loop NOT implemented in Zeebe per bpmn-coverage.md:139.

Pros: compact; one BPMN activity; worker controls the loop. Cons: hides the polling from BPMN readers; worker holds the job activation throughout (long-running worker). Pattern 2: BPMN-level explicit loop ` [Service Task: Check Status] → [XOR Gateway: ready?] → yes → [continue downstream] → no → [Timer Intermediate Event: wait 30s] → [back to Check Status] Pros: visible in BPMN; per-iteration observability (each "Check Status" execution in Operate); explicit timer between attempts. Cons: more elements; back-edge can look messy. Pattern 3: Standard Loop marker (NOT IMPLEMENTED) BPMN spec: with a loop condition. Zeebe does NOT implement Standard Loop per bpmn-coverage.md:127-139 -- lacks the className="implemented" marker. Deployment of activities carrying standardLoopCharacteristics` is rejected. For Camunda 8 production, Pattern 2 (explicit BPMN loop) is most robust + observable; Pattern 1 is compact but hides polling; Pattern 3 cannot be deployed.
